header.title: JobBot v2.4
header.subtitle: Asynchronous prospecting and automated outreach pipeline via national-scale dorking, stealth scraping and dynamic CV compilation
header.author: Alaska Elaina González

cover.alt: JobBot Rich TUI dashboard running in daemon mode, displaying real-time prospecting metrics: processed companies, dispatched emails and average prospect score
cover.caption: JobBot Rich TUI telemetry panel during a continuous execution session. The interface displays the status of each pipeline phase and the cumulative metrics for the active campaign.

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.body: JobBot is an automated job prospecting system designed to circumvent the structural limitations of traditional employment portals. By combining national-scale Google Dorking techniques, WAF-evasion scraping (Cloudflare, Datadome), a deterministic lexical scoring engine and a Typst typographic compilation pipeline, the system autonomously assembles and dispatches hyper-contextualised CVs. This document describes the four-phase pipeline architecture, the technical decisions behind its implementation, the stealth evasion strategies employed, and the operational considerations of a resilient daemon designed for continuous execution.

s1.heading: 1. Motivation and Context
s1.p1: After months of submitting applications through conventional job portals (ZonaJobs, Bumeran, LinkedIn) with response rates below three per cent, the working hypothesis was that the problem lay not in the applicant's profile but in the communication channel. Portals act as intermediaries between candidate and company, filter the CV before it reaches a decision-maker, and introduce multi-week latencies into a process that could be completed in hours.
s1.p2: The conceptual solution was straightforward: eliminate the intermediary and deliver the CV directly to the company, as was done before mass digitisation. The implementation, however, required solving non-trivial technical challenges: how to identify relevant companies at national scale, how to extract contact data from sites that actively resist scraping, and how to personalise each communication sufficiently to avoid being discarded as spam.
s1.p3: When evaluating cold-emailing and OSINT automation tools available on GitHub, a recurring pattern emerged: most existing repositories function as wrappers around paid generative AI APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic) to perform classification tasks that a deterministic lexical engine can execute with greater precision and at zero marginal cost. JobBot was built to fill that gap.

s2.heading: 2. Pipeline Architecture
s2.p1: The system is organised into four sequential phases, each implemented as an independent Python module that reads from and writes to a shared SQLite database (jobbot.db). This design allows each phase to be executed in isolation during development or chained together in continuous daemon mode via the --auto flag.
s2.fig1_alt: Four-phase pipeline flow diagram: Dorking Engine generates target company URLs, the Scraper module extracts and scores contact data, the Dispatcher selects prospects above a minimum score threshold, and the Typst engine compiles and sends the personalised CV
s2.fig1_caption: Data flow between the four pipeline phases. Each module operates asynchronously and can be invoked independently via the CLI.
s2.p2: Phase 1 (Dorking Engine) automates advanced Google queries using industry-sector lists defined in an external file (rubros.txt). Search terms are dynamically constructed by combining the sector with dorking operators that filter for sites with exposed contact data (emails, contact forms, About Us pages). The resulting URLs are stored as seeds in the database with PENDING status.
s2.p3: Phase 2 (Scraping) processes pending seeds using a pool of parallel Playwright browser instances, configurable via the --concurrencia parameter. Each browser operates within an isolated context with active stealth configuration. The module extracts emails, phone numbers and contact names using a set of regular expressions built from structural patterns common to Argentine corporate websites.
s2.p4: Phase 3 (Scoring) applies a custom lexical engine that scores each prospect against criteria including estimated company size, presence of keywords relevant to the candidate's profile, and quality of the extracted contact data. The resulting score (0–100) determines whether the prospect enters the dispatch queue.
s2.p5: Phase 4 (Dispatch) operates over both SMTP and WhatsApp Web. For the email channel, the orchestrator invokes the Typst compiler with keywords extracted from the target company's website, generates a personalised PDF in milliseconds, and attaches it to the email prior to dispatch.

s3.heading: 3. Stealth Evasion Techniques
s3.p1: A significant proportion of Argentine corporate websites operate behind WAF protection systems such as Cloudflare or bot-detection services such as Datadome. Bypassing these protections without engaging in illegal conduct requires emulating, with sufficient fidelity, the navigation pattern of a real human user.
s3.p2: The stealth module combines four complementary strategies. First, playwright-stealth patches the navigator object properties used by anti-bot systems as fingerprinting vectors (WebDriver flag, plugins, languages). Second, browser context rotation is applied: each request is executed in a fresh context with a user-agent string randomly selected from a corpus of real strings captured from production traffic. Third, timezone spoofing aligns the browser's temporal profile with the geolocation of the target server. Finally, human biometrics are simulated via randomised mouse movements and scroll events interpolated using Bezier curves before any DOM interaction.
s3.fig2_alt: Chrome DevTools panel capture showing navigator object properties without the WebDriver markers that anti-bot systems detect, confirming that the stealth patch is effective
s3.fig2_caption: Inspection of the navigator object in a Playwright context with active stealth. The absence of the webdriver: true property and the presence of real plugins confirm that the fingerprint passes basic detection controls.
s3.p3: The system additionally implements an adaptive jitter mechanism: between each request, a random pause is introduced whose duration follows a uniform distribution between configurable values, preventing the scraping's temporal pattern from being detectable via statistical analysis of inter-request intervals.

s4.heading: 4. Dynamic CV Engine with Typst
s4.p1: JobBot's primary differentiator over conventional cold-emailing solutions is its ability to generate a unique PDF document per recipient at runtime. Typst is a modern typographic composition system that compiles documents from its own markup language to PDF in under 200 ms, making it feasible to invoke within the dispatch loop.
s4.p2: The personalisation process operates in three steps. First, the orchestrator issues a GET request to the target company's main page and extracts the most frequent terms from visible content using the same lexical engine as the scoring module. Second, these terms are injected as variables into the Typst template (cvs/template.typ) via parameter substitution. Third, the Typst binary is invoked as a subprocess via subprocess.run, and the resulting PDF is written to a temporary directory before being attached to the email.
s4.p3: The result is a document whose professional summary and skills description reference terminology specific to the recipient's industry and company, increasing the open rate and reducing the probability of automatic spam classification.

s5.heading: 5. 24/7 Daemon and Operational Considerations
s5.p1: In --auto mode, JobBot executes the full cycle (Dork → Scrape → Mail) in an infinite loop managed by asyncio. The process is designed to run under tmux or as a systemd service. Daemon resilience is ensured through three mechanisms: explicit POSIX signal handling (SIGTERM, SIGINT) for a safe shutdown without database corruption; strict configurable timeouts per phase to prevent livelocks against unresponsive sites; and a 90-day per-domain cooldown stored in the campanas_envios table, preventing repeated outreach to the same contact.
s5.p2: The SMTP dispatch limit is configured to respect Gmail's App Passwords rate limits (500 emails/day). For higher volume, the SMTP module can be configured against a dedicated mail server or a transactional relay without any modifications to the business logic.

header.category: Security Audit · Responsible Disclosure · OWASP Top 10
header.title: Security Audit — Employment Platform
header.subtitle: Discovery and responsible disclosure of nine critical and high-severity vulnerabilities in a public employment system, including Broken Authentication, IDOR, BOLA, Unrestricted File Upload and potential Stored XSS
header.author: Alaska Elaina González

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.body: This document describes the discovery, analysis and responsible disclosure process for nine critical and high-severity vulnerabilities in a publicly accessible employment platform. The initial finding was accidental: it arose while completing my profile on the portal after receiving a response from the company via JobBot. From an anomaly in date persistence, the investigation expanded to reveal structural flaws that allowed the extraction, corruption and impersonation of records belonging to all registered users. The report was delivered to the organisation's technical team in accordance with responsible disclosure principles.

s1.heading: 1. How It Started: From an Automated Email to a Security Finding
s1.p1: Everything began in the most mundane way possible. JobBot, the B2B prospecting pipeline I develop in parallel, had automatically dispatched my CV to a company in the sector. Days later I received a genuine response: they invited me to complete my profile on their proprietary recruitment portal to continue with the selection process.
s1.p2: I entered the portal, began filling out the form, and upon reaching the date fields — graduation year, work experience start date — I noticed something unusual: the system was persisting the year 2026 as 2022. This was not a display issue; the value stored on the server was incorrect.
s1.p3: By instinct, I opened the browser's developer tools and inspected the DOM. What I found was immediate and self-evident: the value attributes of the year-selector <option> elements were incorrectly assigned in the HTML itself. By modifying them directly from the console and resubmitting the form, the server accepted and persisted the corrected value without any additional validation.
s1.p4: This confirmed that all validation logic resided exclusively on the client side. The server accepted whatever value arrived in the request body, regardless of semantic validity. Curious about the extent of this permissiveness, I tested the following: the month field accepted values from 1 to 12. I changed the selector's value to 13 and submitted the form.
s1.p5: The server processed the request without error. My profile was left completely corrupted in the database. What had begun as a cosmetic bug fix had become a demonstration of a generalised server-side input validation failure. And that was only the beginning.
s1.p6: Since the server did not validate input data, the logical next question was: did it also fail to validate the requestor's identity? I opened DevTools, navigated to the Application tab and inspected localStorage. There it was: a JSON object containing my user id and a logeado: true field. I modified the id to that of another user, reloaded the page, and the system presented that person's complete profile as if it were my own — without a password, without a token, without any server-side verification whatsoever. From that point on, the audit was inevitable.

s2.heading: 2. Analysis Methodology
s2.p1: Once the absence of server-side controls was confirmed, the investigation was structured into two phases. The first focused on frontend analysis: session behaviour, identifier exposure and resource access policies. The second centred on the backend: API endpoints, file handling, HTTP methods in use and exposed documentation.
s2.p2: All tests were conducted exclusively on my own account. In cases where access to third-party data was verified, no records were extracted, stored or disclosed. The objective at all times was to document the attack surface, not to exploit it. The proof-of-concept evidence included in the report is sufficient to reproduce the findings in a controlled environment without accessing real user data.

s3.heading: 3. Findings — Phase One: Frontend
s3.flaw1_p1: The platform's authentication mechanism did not exist on the server side. The session was represented as a localStorage object of the form { id: N, logeado: true }. The server treated any request that arrived with a user ID as a valid authenticated session, without verifying tokens, secure cookies or credentials of any kind.
s3.flaw1_p2: The proof of concept consisted of two lines executed in the browser console:
s3.flaw1_p3: This finding is the foundation upon which all access-control vulnerabilities identified in the system rest. Without real server-side authentication, no other control can be effective.
s3.flaw2_title: Finding 2 — IDOR: Unauthorised Record Access (OWASP A01:2021) — CRITICAL
s3.flaw2_p1: User identifiers were sequential and predictable numeric values. Combining the session impersonation described in Finding 1 with iteration over the observed ID range, any external actor could access the entirety of registered profiles. Each profile exposed full name, photograph, national ID number, email, phone number, exact residential address, CV in PDF format, and complete employment and academic history, among other data.
s3.flaw2_p2: The absence of identifier obfuscation, combined with the lack of ownership validation on each request, meant that full database extraction was achievable via basic automation within hours, without generating significant alerts in network monitoring.

s4.heading: 4. Findings — Phase Two: Backend
s4.flaw3_title: Finding 3 — Unrestricted File Upload (OWASP A04:2021) — CRITICAL
s4.flaw3_p1: The image and document upload endpoints did not verify file extension, MIME type or internal structure (Magic Bytes). The ability to host executable scripts disguised as Base64-encoded images was confirmed. An attacker with access to the system could exploit this surface to execute arbitrary code on the server infrastructure.
s4.flaw4_title: Finding 4 — BOLA: Unauthorised Record Modification (OWASP A01:2021) — CRITICAL
s4.flaw4_p1: The profile update endpoint processed PUT requests relying exclusively on the id field in the JSON body, without verifying that the requestor was the legitimate owner of the resource. Any authenticated user — or one simulating authentication — could overwrite the complete profile of any other user, including replacing the associated CV with a malicious resource.
s4.flaw5_title: Finding 5 — Credentials Exposed in URL (OWASP A02:2021) — HIGH
s4.flaw5_p1: The login mechanism operated via the HTTP GET method, transmitting the email address and password as parameters in the query string. This practice exposes credentials in the browser history, web server logs and any intermediate network node.
s4.flaw6_title: Finding 6 — Internal Documentation Exposed in Production — MEDIUM
s4.flaw6_p1: The OpenAPI specification (swagger.yaml) was publicly accessible in the production environment without any access control. This document provides a complete map of all services, schemas and API parameters, significantly reducing the effort required for an attacker to map the attack surface.
s4.flaw7_title: Finding 7 — Potential Stored XSS in Filename (OWASP A03:2021) — MEDIUM
s4.flaw7_p1: The system stored the original filenames of uploaded files in the database without applying any server-side sanitisation. HTML and JavaScript injection payloads in filenames were confirmed to be persisted correctly:
s4.flaw8_title: Finding 8 — Absent Business Logic and Input Validation (OWASP A04:2021) — MEDIUM
s4.flaw8_p1: As described in the introductory section, form field constraints existed only on the client side. The server accepted and persisted semantically invalid values — such as month 13 — without emitting validation errors. Additionally, by manipulating HTTP responses, internal server traces were obtained that revealed implementation details useful to an attacker.
s4.flaw9_p1: The underlying web server had reached its end-of-life date more than twelve months prior to the audit. The infrastructure was operating without critical security patches from the vendor, substantially amplifying the risk posed by the code execution vulnerabilities identified in Finding 3.

s5.heading: 5. Scope of Exposed Data
s5.p1: Each record accessible through the described vulnerabilities exposed the following personal information for every registered user on the platform: profile photograph, full name, national identity document number, email address, gender and date of birth, telephone numbers, exact residential address, geographic zone, professional profile links, CV in PDF format, academic qualifications, language competencies and employment history.
s5.p2: The applicable regulatory framework includes local personal data protection legislation. The combination of unique identifiers (national ID, email, phone) with precise location data and employment documentation represents a high risk of fraudulent misuse in the event of exposure.

s6.heading: 6. Suggested Remediation Plan
s6.intro: The report delivered to the technical team included the following corrective actions, ordered by priority:
s6.auth_body: implement server-side session management using signed JWTs or HttpOnly / Secure cookies, eliminating any reliance on localStorage for user identity verification.
s6.ac_body: verify on every request that the resource identifier being manipulated strictly corresponds to the validated session of the requestor, rejecting any cross-access attempt.
s6.ids_body: replace predictable numeric sequences with universally unique identifiers (UUID v4) to eliminate the possibility of profile enumeration and scraping.
s6.files_body: establish allowlists of permitted formats and cryptographically verify the actual structure of each uploaded file via Magic Byte analysis, rejecting any upload that does not pass both validations.
s6.api_body: migrate the login endpoint to POST, transmitting credentials exclusively in the encrypted request body (TLS). Remove the OpenAPI specification from the production environment or protect it with authentication.
s6.infra_body: plan the urgent migration of servers to an operating system and web server with active vendor support, prioritising the application of outstanding security patches.

s7.heading: 7. Responsible Disclosure Process
s7.p1: Once the findings were documented, I compiled a complete technical report and delivered it directly to the affected organisation's internal team. Throughout the investigation, no mass data extraction was performed, no third-party records were modified beyond the minimum proof-of-concept evidence required to verify each vulnerability, and no information belonging to other users was disclosed or stored.
s7.p2: The objective of this work was exclusively to enable the organisation to identify and remediate the issues before they could be discovered and exploited by malicious actors. The distribution of the full technical report is restricted to the organisation's internal technical team.
s7.p3: This public case study deliberately omits the name of the affected organisation, the exact observed ID ranges, the complete endpoint paths and any other data that could facilitate reproduction of the attacks against production systems.

header.title: INVARIANT
header.subtitle: Decentralised forensic infrastructure suite designed to intercept and evaluate computational hardware operating at privileged execution rings, sustained by four cryptographically interconnected pillars
header.author: Alaska Elaina González

abstract.title: Abstract
abstract.body: INVARIANT operates as a decentralised forensic infrastructure suite designed to intercept and evaluate computational hardware operating at privileged execution rings (Ring-0). The system rests on four highly decoupled yet cryptographically interconnected pillars: a Control Plane built in Go (invariant-api) handling state management and asymmetric credential issuance; a high-performance SvelteKit interface for operational control (invariant-web); an asynchronous Rust/Tauri engine for NAND memory interaction (invariant_flasher); and a live, self-contained Python/Arch Linux forensic probe for deterministic bare-metal extraction (prob-tex).

s1.heading: 1. Motivation and Context
s1.p1: Modern hardware audits suffer from a systemic blind spot when relying on the host Operating System (Windows/macOS). A user-space system inherently lies: WMI returns cached metrics, drivers collapse under stress, and damaged sectors are abstracted away by OS heuristics. The motivation behind INVARIANT is to establish a Zero-Trust validation mechanism that rejects all mediation from commercial operating systems.
s1.p2: By directly seizing ownership of hardware resources (ACPI, MSR, PCIe, NVMe), it becomes possible to distil the underlying silicon reality. This approach turns a USB drive into a destructive probe of illusions, providing a relentless interface for hardware reliability engineering.

s2.heading: 2. Pipeline Architecture
s2.p1: INVARIANT's orchestration forms an uninterrupted closed loop. The flow begins at the Control Plane, where invariant-api exposes asset telemetry and low-latency S3 pre-signed tokens through invariant-web's Edge reverse proxy. The frontend provides state visibility while the operator downloads and launches invariant_flasher. This program compiles an on-demand ISO generated by forge.sh, destroying partitions on physical media and burning the local boot image together with an asymmetrically signed licence seed. Finally, the prob-tex probe boots from the Air-Gapped environment, executing entirely outside the radar and constraints of host systems.
s2.probtex_p1: Python engine on immutable Arch Linux. Performs block-level introspection by invoking kernel calls (lsblk) to map the bus (NVMe/SATA) while ignoring logical volumes, ensuring raw and sequential I/O without host OS interference.
s2.api_p1: Constructs a canonical JSON payload and signs it with an Ed25519 elliptic-curve key over raw UTF-8 bytes, guaranteeing that licensing data is immutable and verifiable in a fully offline (air-gapped) environment.
s2.web_p1: Hijacks the network topology by operating a server-side reverse proxy (Cloudflare Workers) that mutates and masks requests, bypassing CORS asymmetries through a strict single-origin bridge for the HTTPOnly session.
s2.flasher_p1: Acquires a raw file descriptor directly to the device silicon by injecting the O_SYNC kernel flag, short-circuiting in-memory cache heuristics to guarantee that the ISO overwrites NAND transistors synchronously.

s3.heading: 3. Cryptography and Access Control
s3.p1: The decoupling between cloud and the isolated device demands robust cryptography to mitigate impersonation attacks. Hardware authorisation is encapsulated locally without requiring internet access. The central entity (invariant-api) employs crypto/ed25519 to sign a LicensePayload with deterministic parameters injected into the JSON structure, anchoring absolute expiry dates (Time-Trap) that depend on the HWID generated from the SHA-256 hash of the host's DMI identifiers.
s3.p2: This tokenisation includes a synchronous kill-switch: if the asymmetric proof of the token is rejected, prob-tex immediately triggers a virtual kernel-panic, permanently blocking the forensic report and preventing any data exfiltration in a hostile environment.

s4.heading: 4. Low-Level Evasion and Flashing
s4.p1: Deploying a live OS while evading hostile restrictions requires manipulating the local partition table while bypassing buffers. The invariant_flasher module is built in Rust and employs native OS bindings to force an exclusive open (O_SYNC on POSIX and FILE_FLAG_WRITE_THROUGH on Win32) of the block descriptor /dev/sdX or \\.\PhysicalDriveX.
s4.p2: The subsystem heuristically destroys the MBR, primary GPT, and even the Backup GPT located at binary level in the last LBA sectors (17,408 bytes), eliminating potential mount collisions or 'resurrections' guided by tools such as parted or gdisk, before sequentially injecting the Arch Linux boot block.

s5.heading: 5. The Bare-Metal Forensic Engine
s5.p1: The core of truth resides in prob-tex: a subsystem that runs multiple threads under a strict ThreadPoolExecutor, capable of bypassing xHCI bus delays for USB and SATA/NVMe latencies by iterating block nodes directly (lsblk -J). Raw metadata is extracted by invoking fio, smartctl, and parallel stress-ng spikes on RAM/Motherboard, with sequential runs (due to bus constraints) on CPU/GPU.
s5.p2: The computed entropy index cannot be injected or modified by the hostile ACPI interface; it is protected by sanitising LaTeX strings from BIOS DMI blocks (preventing LaTeX injection attacks), finally dumping an immutable PDF document by invoking the off-grid tectonic rendering binary through ISO-level pre-loaded caches.

s6.heading: 6. Internal Ecosystem Audit
s6.friction_p: The primary stress zone falls on the synchronous I/O bottleneck during media writing (flashing) and hardware enumeration. Abstractions such as udevadm settle on Linux, or the FILE_FLAG_WRITE_THROUGH streams of Windows in the flasher, impose severe timeouts. If the xHCI bus or the physical device fails to synchronise asynchronously with kernel descriptors, silent failures or race conditions arise that collapse the entire pipe, forcing the flasher to apply static waits as a defensive tactic.
s6.zerotrust_p: The ecosystem's design assumes a threat vector where no endpoint is trusted. The host OS is considered deeply compromised and is therefore ignored; the system injects its own bare-metal environment based on immutable Linux (prob-tex) to evaluate thermal and wear telemetry. Web client assertions are never trusted, with subscription expiries and grace periods revalidated through session interception in the backend (user_assets / hardware_trials). BIOS and reported hardware are treated as potentially malicious inputs, with rigorous LaTeX injection sanitisation applied in prob-tex's main.py when reading DMI fields.

s7.heading: 7. Operational Considerations
s7.p1: INVARIANT requires tolerance for and mitigation design against stochastic local hardware failures, such as unstable drives or decaying USB controllers. To this end, strict fallback mechanisms are implemented (e.g. falling back to /dev/nvme0n1 subsystems if PCIe detection collapses) and silent firstboot locks through aggressive masking (systemd-firstboot suppression at initramfs level).
s7.p2: This ecosystem turns a USB drive into a destructive probe of illusions, providing a relentless interface for hardware reliability engineering and ensuring deterministic integrity against underlying silicon asymmetries.
